deck: Dr. Madeleine Ballard of the Community Health Impact Coalition on the proCHW movement: making community health workers salaried, skilled, supervised, and supplied the global norm, and what that means for technologists and funders.
seo_description: Dr. Madeleine Ballard of CHIC on the proCHW movement to make community health workers salaried, skilled, supervised, and supplied the norm worldwide.
og_description: Dr. Madeleine Ballard on the proCHW movement to make community health workers salaried, skilled, supervised, and supplied.
about: Community health workers are proven, again and again, to be the best way to get care to the poor, the rural, the stigmatized, and the underserved. Yet millions are not salaried, skilled, supervised, or supplied. The WHO recommends remunerating CHWs, but more than 80 percent of those in Africa are unsalaried, and most are women. CHWs also face essential medicine stock-outs about a third of the time, leaving them less able to treat common causes of illness and death like malaria, diarrhea, and pneumonia.

Dr. Madeleine Ballard, CEO of the Community Health Impact Coalition (CHIC), joins Amie Vaccaro and Jonathan Jackson to make the case for professional CHWs (proCHWs). CHIC is a coalition of CHWs and aligned health organizations in about 40 countries working to make professional CHWs the norm by changing guidelines, funding, and policy. Ballard explains the countries leading the way, why female labor has been treated as cheap and optional, and how CHIC's policy dashboard tracks progress across the 137 countdown countries.

The conversation also confronts the role of technology, which Ballard insists is never neutral. Used well it can make health workers' jobs better, surface and dignify their labor, and even grow the movement, as a free digital advocacy course has done by reaching thousands of CHWs. Used poorly it becomes extractive. The episode closes with a clear call to action: join the movement for proCHWs so that everyone, including those who provide care, has the support they need.

pull_quote.text: There hasn't historically been an organization of community health workers, this idea that they haven't been at the table. And so, as we know, that means you're on the menu.

bullets: The 4 S's framework: why CHWs must be salaried, skilled, supervised, and supplied, and how far reality falls short
Inside CHIC: a coalition of CHWs and aligned organizations in about 40 countries changing guidelines, funding, and policy
The policy dashboard: tracking which of the 137 countdown countries actually accredit and pay community health workers
Why the choice isn't free: how poverty, gender norms, and a boiling-the-frog promise of pay keep CHWs working unpaid
Technology is not neutral: avoiding extractive tools and building ones that make health workers' labor visible and dignified
Partner, not vendor: Dimagi's stake in the ground that technology must focus on creating better jobs for CHWs
A call to action: how technologists, funders, implementers, and policymakers can join the proCHW movement
